cards.body: Gusto means 'want' or 'like.' It's how you express preferences.

example.english: I want it. / I like it.
example.note: Gusto ko = I want. Ko here means 'I' (not 'my'). Context tells you which.

alam_mo_ba_inline.fact: Gusto comes from Spanish 'gusto' (taste/pleasure). The pure Tagalog word is 'ibig' but almost nobody uses it anymore.

cards.body: Na means 'already' or 'now.' It changes the timing of what you're saying.

example.with_na: Gusto ko na. (I want it now / already.)
example.note: Na makes things immediate or completed.

cards.body: Lang means 'just' or 'only.' It softens or limits what you're asking for.

example.without: Gusto ko ng tubig. (I want water.)
example.with_lang: Gusto ko na lang ng tubig. (I just want water.)
example.note: Na lang together = 'just' or 'only' (softened). Very common in Filipino conversation.

cards.explanation: Gusto ko na lang = I just want. Ng kape = coffee.

cards.source_text: You just want water. Nothing else.
cards.explanation: Lang = just/only. Gusto ko na lang = I just want.

cards.source_text: What does 'na' add to a sentence?
cards.explanation: Na = already/now. 'Kumain ka na' = you already ate. 'Gusto ko na' = I want it now.
Gusto ko na lang po ng tubig. Po for respect, na lang for 'just.'

wrong_answer_tracking.behaviour: Wrong answers go to back of current session queue.

alam_mo_ba: 'Na lang' together is one of the most common Filipino expressions. It softens any request: 'Isa na lang po' = just one please.
Na is incredibly versatile. 'Kumain ka na' (you already ate), 'mabuti na' (it's fine now), 'gusto ko na' (I want it now).
Lang can also express modesty. 'Student lang ako' = I'm just a student. Filipinos use it to downplay themselves.
